Actually you don't need any firmware mod at all, in most slicing programs you can just define the diameter of the nozzle in the settings and it will do the rest for you.
Cura is a free slicing program that has basic settings for beginners and a lot of advanced stuff for anyone who wants it, and it renders much faster than the other software around.

author: @mentibump
text:
The concept of liquefied rather than melted plastic opens a "possibilities" thought bubble. 

In a dual extruder system, where the outer layer is done with "fine" liquified deposition, could help overcome the unsightly layering of heat deposition.  (Plus the acetone based methods people use to smooth it out.) -- Decrease extrusion time...

Cold liquified extrusion opens the Education / School opportunities with less OH&S to worry about than the heat based methods. Also no warping through differential heat retention.  Less warp vs more shrinkage.

I think the closest to your concept in current use is Chocolate 3D printing.  There are a number of devices being used.  An overly expensive dedicated plunger extruder for chocolate is at:
http://www.alibaba.com/product-detail/chocolate-3d-printer_1835343358.html

A number of 3D printers also extrude epoxy onto ever increasing layers of powdered material "binder jetting" and even sheets of paper (Mcor IRIS) to build up an object. That object is then "broken out" of the non-treated / extra material.

Look forward to your further videos on the subject

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is expensive - I am going to build one myself I think - well not  just me lol - I am only a chemist mate

author: @larrypenoux9065
text: This is a brilliant idea. A couple of points to address: Dry time for the liquid will need to be very fast. A heated print bed might do well to speed up the dry time but may also introduce warping depending on how the liquid reacts to temperature gradients through the print process. Assuming that the needle won't clog up getting near the heated bed, the only other detail I can think of is pump flow vs. deposition/dry time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cheers mate - I was thinking that - but they deposit clay, chocolate and sugar with this method so my thought is to add a filler powder to get sufficient 'stiffnes' until drying was complete. Filler up to 60% is a normal addition to production plastics

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text:
I had a similar idea quite some time ago but I thought I was crazy and assumed that it wouldn't work for some reason.

where you thinking about making some of the inks conductive? That would be easy. What would be a little more challenging but also interesting would be to have a semiconductor as well. if you could attenuate the conductivity of the conductive ink you have a resistor, the semiconductor for transistors, not much more to make a (very limited and inefficient) computer you can print into all sorts of objects.

one consequence of this could be that you could tell the device to make a copy of itself!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It may well be crazy but it's worth a look lol - I already make conductive inks and istrated this from making conductive filaments - have a look at the earlier vids - but essentially you are right though i suspect you underestimate a bit about how 'easy' it is lol

replies.author: @keeganmaloney3440
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Sorry I didn't mean to belittle. The process is involved, I more meant the conductive inks seemed eminently doable considering you have already been working on it. By comparison, I have no idea how to make a semiconductor ink. In fact, you would have to make 2, one n-type, and one p-type. I'm not sure how well they would work on the mm scale either. there are a lot of challenges, including potentially much higher energy requirements to change the transistor state.

if we assume we can make 1mm deposits within an accuracy within about 100 micrometers, you could put one p-type dot down, one n-type on each side, you could use the conductive ink for the gate and a less conductive or possibly fully insulating ink to keep the gate from bridging across the depletion zone. It seems theoretically sound. 

With this kind of work though, you would want to isolate the device from vibrations or even disturbances in the air, wouldn't you?

replies.author: @keeganmaloney3440
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith side note, if you can make the n-type and p-type inks, you have all you need to make a p-n junction diode as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Keegan Maloney
It would be a real challenge mate - but definately interesting - the pn types aren't too difficult and a proof of concept might be to print a peltier device. - But lets get abs dissloved first lol

author: @roidroid
text:
There are some peristaltic pumps on Thingiverse, some specifically designed for paste extruding 3d printers.
eg: http://youtu.be/axAP0btw4Lk

Since others are chatting i'll chime in too: my favourite 3D printer is the open source *Gus Simpson*  http://youtu.be.com/IEGTe1G2Gqs
Unlike most printers this one has no metal rails or screw rods, which saves money.  It's design gives it quite a large build area for it's size - it can print larger versions of itself.  It's still quite new and experimental though, there seems to be very few of them in the wild as of yet, but i'm smitten by what i've seen.  This thing looks cool.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: So - what I am thinking is not a million miles away from what is already being done - that is actually very good to know

author: @roidroid
text:
One specific advantage this would have over filament 3d printers is that actual filament diameter is never exact, the variability can be wild.  Since the extruder on a 3d printer works by forcibly ramming the solid filament into the extruder, this means that the amount of melted material comming outof the hot nozzle is also inexact.  This imposes an upper resolution limit on all filament 3d printers.

Since your liquid plastic feedstock remains a liquid throughout, the actual volumes extruded will likely be more predictable (when compared to filament extruding).

author: @power-max
text: How would you prevent the liquid plastic from hardening? I would be afraid that as the device is printing, the liquid solution would begin to harden inside of the capillary and render is useless and unreliable. Also, I assume that this solution is not heated to keep it liquefied, as then it would be not much different than the current design. The only way I could imagine keeping the plastic liquified is by chemical means. It cannot be based on a time and half life for reasons described above, so it would have to be 2 separate chemicals that instantly react together like epoxy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Not at all. It dries just like an inkjet does now - there is very little difference

replies.author: @billbrown4466
replies.text: there have been similar machine like this for years look up the mark 10 polyurethane machine the resin catalyst and colour are all mixed at the print head and the rate of curing is controlled by temperature the hotter the print head the faster the chemical reaction the faster it hardens up, of course when I was using this machine we were using it to cast urethane parts, the mold pre heated and then poured put back in the oven to harden up, then de-moulded and placed back in the oven to cure over night. some of the parts we made were for a mining machinery company and aerospace as well this stuff was strong and came in a large variety of shore hardness and even foam balls that remind me of those balls they used to play dodgeball with in elementary school,

author: @directeeccks
text:
There are a lot of options for 3d printing techniques out there that all have there benefits/methods/materials. Since this is a different kind of material then the most common 3d printers use the amount of useful info is going to be hard to find.  From what I gather the plastic will have conductivity and would be good to thing to keep in mind during the design process.  Which only a few printers out there on the market print conductive materials. There is a printer for circuits called the Ex1 that you could take a look at. 

The biggest challenge I see with this is extrusion, extrusion rate, and programing of your micro controllers.  It is really going to have to be a lot of trial and error to get the timing right on extrusion rate.  The other thing to point out use a tubing that connects to the printer so the motors don't have to pull the weight of the plastic, giving your prints more stability, and giving you the ability to design a higher volume storage.
You could have a tank that is off to the side feeding the tube.  I am not quite sure how to go about adding controlled pressure to push for extrusion though. 

Then I would focus on getting just getting x and y stable.  Then pursue z afterwords.  I think this would be a good application for a 2d print to start cause even in 2d it would provide a fairly unique and useful service for circuit creation.   Then when it goes to 3d to not use the liquid plastic as a structure but use it in conjunction traditional plastic 3d printing method.  This would be a dual head design that would print the structure  and the plastic circuit layout printed into it. You could also print the inner workings with tradition plastic printing and use the higher resolution liquid plastic on the outsides of the print to increase production times and keep high resolution. It would lend itself to some pretty unique designs.  There is a program that uses legos as the inner workings to shorten the times of prints called faBrickator.

Anyways cheers and sorry for being long winded XD

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It was very helpful - thanks. The plastic doesn't need to be conductive and changing it would be a matter of selecting print head. I like your ideas of a dual print and the feed system you suggest - like an inkjet CISS system? - I suspect i will have to for an all in one design though as people don't like doing a job twice and a 'press the button and leave it is going' to be far more popular for most people - my plan was more to modify and existing filament printer so i would expect xy problems to be solved but your point of z axis stability is well taken. I think you are 100% right about the problem of extrusion speed is going to be a killer here and that is where i think most effort needs to be put. Someone has suggested pneumatics for the feed system and I hadn't thought of that but i like it. Thanks for your input mate - Rob

replies.author: @directeeccks
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith No problem.  I am glad I could help.  I know very little in terms of material chemistry and you have helped me tons.

Here is a little more info.   From what I gathered the size you wanted to achieve is around 100 microns.(a diabetic needles is about 130 microns or so)  The current top self prebuilt traditional 3d printers that are meant for the consumer market print around 100 microns and sale for around 2 to 3 k MakerBots. There is one that boast the highest resolution yet which is the  HD^2 at 25 microns and it was going to be cheaper. They had a kickstarter that unfortunately didn't reach it's goal.  Those are good examples to look at as far as parts and setup.  It will help give you a reference to work from when choosing your parts.  The other place to look for invaluable info is the RepRap forums.  There are a ton of post the in there that may fill some of the gaps and lead you further towards your working prototype. There is a few post with some "out of the box thinking" type builds too.  Plus all the woes of sourcing parts, coding and compatibility issues. anyways... If you can get prints at 100 microns or less with dual heads and new materials it will be one hell of a diy first build.
Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
directeeccks
thanks for that mate - off over to the reprap forums now lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: as a software issue I imagine that would be an easy solve - I was thinking along the lines of an old plotter where three or four 'pens' would be used on higher grade machines. or you could code the computer model and a single 'pen' would come to rest you'd change it and press a 'carry on' button - at least that's what i was thinking

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text:
None of that would be terribly hard to implement. Tricky bits are things like having nozzles not get blocked after use, and making them home exactly the same every time.

You can also get a range of sizes from one nozzle depending on pressure, viscosity, and movement speed.
I am using 0.3mm nozzles on my filament printer, they can in theroy extrude anywhere from 0.1mm to 2mm beads. Depending on how the Heat, filment feed rate, and movment speed are set.
